_note: SEED DATA — all restaurant names, addresses, phone numbers, and hours below are for development purposes only. Please verify and update with accurate, current details before any public release.

cities.name: Toronto
cities.description: Canada's largest city and home to one of the biggest Filipino communities, centred in Scarborough and Mississauga.

cities.name: Vancouver
cities.description: A vibrant Filipino community with excellent restaurants spread across the Lower Mainland.

cities.name: Montréal
cities.description: A growing Filipino food scene in Canada's cultural capital.

cities.name: Calgary
cities.description: Alberta's largest city with a thriving Filipino community and a growing restaurant scene.

cities.name: Kelowna
cities.description: A tight-knit Filipino food community in the beautiful Okanagan.

cities.name: Winnipeg
cities.description: One of the highest concentrations of Filipino Canadians per capita — and the food scene shows it.

cities.name: Edmonton
cities.description: A large and well-established Filipino community with excellent authentic restaurants.

cities.name: Ottawa
cities.description: The nation's capital has a close-knit Filipino community with charming home-style gems.

restaurants.name: Ihaw-Ihaw Restaurant
restaurants.blurb: A Scarborough institution beloved for its crispy lechon and smoky grilled meats that taste just like home.
restaurants.description: Ihaw-Ihaw has been a cornerstone of the Scarborough Filipino community for over a decade. The pit-roasted lechon draws crowds from across the GTA, and for good reason — the skin is perfectly crackling and the meat is tender throughout. Their sisig, served sizzling on a cast-iron plate, is a must-order.
restaurants.whyFavorite: The whole lechon sells out by mid-afternoon on weekends — call ahead for fiesta catering. It's the first stop for anyone craving authentic grilled Filipino meats in Toronto.

restaurants.name: Kayal Filipino Cuisine
restaurants.blurb: Unpretentious Scarborough gem serving hearty sinigang and kare-kare that rivals your lola's cooking.
restaurants.description: Kayal is the kind of place you come back to week after week. The sinigang na baboy is sour and fragrant, cooked to order with just the right balance. The kare-kare comes with a generous portion of oxtail and a rich peanut sauce that goes perfectly with bagoong. Cash-friendly and always welcoming.
restaurants.whyFavorite: The sinigang here has that perfect sour-broth balance that's hard to find outside a Filipino home kitchen. Portions are generous and the price is unbeatable.

restaurants.name: Tito Rey's Turo-Turo
restaurants.blurb: Point-and-eat turo-turo style with rotating daily specials — the closest thing to eating at a Nanay's kitchen.
restaurants.description: Turo-turo means 'point-point' — you walk up to the steam table, point at what you want, and they pile it onto rice. Tito Rey's rotates its selection daily, but the pork adobo and dinuguan are constants. The bulalo (beef bone marrow soup) on cold Toronto days is pure comfort.
restaurants.whyFavorite: This is turo-turo at its best — fast, affordable, and cooked the traditional way. The rotating menu means there's always something new to try, and the bulalo is among the best in the city.

restaurants.name: Barrio Filipino
restaurants.blurb: Chef-driven modern Filipino in the heart of downtown — elevated classics that honour tradition without being stuffy.
restaurants.description: Barrio Filipino brings a chef's perspective to the cuisine, reimagining classics like kare-kare with premium oxtail and a housemade peanut-coconut sauce, or a crispy lechon belly with vinegar dipping salsa. The halo-halo dessert is a showstopper — layered, generous, and finished tableside. Perfect for date nights or introducing the cuisine to friends.
restaurants.whyFavorite: One of the few elevated Filipino dining experiences in Toronto. The kare-kare rivals anything in Manila and the cocktail list takes cues from Filipino ingredients like calamansi and pandan.

restaurants.name: Max's Restaurant
restaurants.blurb: The beloved Philippine chain, now in Mississauga — the fried chicken is legendary for a reason.
restaurants.description: Max's is a household name in the Philippines, and the Mississauga location carries on that legacy. Their signature fried chicken is impossibly crispy on the outside and juicy within — marinated for hours before frying. The combo platters for groups are great value, and the halo-halo is a proper version with ube ice cream and leche flan.
restaurants.whyFavorite: For many Filipino-Canadians, Max's is a taste of home. The fried chicken is consistently excellent and the portions are made for sharing. Great for big family lunches.

restaurants.name: Kultura Filipino Bistro
restaurants.blurb: A lively bistro near downtown that does Filipino comfort food with style — the sisig and pancit are crowd favourites.
restaurants.description: Kultura strikes a great balance between casual and polished. The sisig comes in multiple variations — pork, chicken, and seafood — all served sizzling. Their pancit canton with crispy garlic is a highlight, and the halo-halo is generous enough for two. A comfortable spot for lunch meetings or laid-back dinners.
restaurants.whyFavorite: The sisig trio platter is the best way to taste all three versions. Kultura is the go-to recommendation for non-Filipino colleagues curious about the cuisine.

restaurants.name: Amber Filipino Restaurant
restaurants.blurb: Family-run Scarborough spot best known for its warming bulalo soup and affordable lunch specials.
restaurants.description: Amber is run by a Filipino family who put heart into every pot. The bulalo — a rich bone marrow and beef shank soup — is the star, ladled out into deep bowls with corn and cabbage. The lunch specials with rice and a viand are among the most affordable in Scarborough. Come hungry.
restaurants.whyFavorite: The bulalo here is soul-restoring on a cold day. Portions are large, staff are warm, and prices are honest. A neighbourhood treasure.

restaurants.name: Lutong Bahay
restaurants.blurb: Literally 'home cooking' — and that's exactly what you get. This North York gem is the real deal.
restaurants.description: Lutong Bahay translates to 'home-cooked food' and the name says it all. The menu is a rotating selection of Filipino home classics — kare-kare, pork adobo, dinuguan, and arroz caldo — cooked fresh each morning. The dinuguan (pork blood stew) here is rich, savoury, and not for the faint of heart, but absolutely delicious.
restaurants.whyFavorite: This is the closest thing to eating at a Filipino grandmother's house in Toronto. The kare-kare with shrimp paste is impeccably balanced and the prices are a gift.

restaurants.name: Jeepney
restaurants.blurb: Named after the iconic Filipino transport, Jeepney brings bold Filipino flavours to funky Kensington Market.
restaurants.description: Jeepney is equal parts restaurant and Filipino cultural experience. The walls are covered in Filipino art and the music sets the mood. Their adobo comes in both dry and saucy versions — the dry adobo floss on rice is addictive. A great selection of Filipino craft drinks pairs well with the sisig.
restaurants.whyFavorite: The lechon belly with vinegar is exceptional, and the vibe in Kensington makes this one of the most fun Filipino dining experiences in Toronto.

restaurants.name: Pinoy Kitchen Brampton
restaurants.blurb: Affordable and filling Filipino comfort food in Brampton — the pancit bihon is made fresh every day.
restaurants.description: Pinoy Kitchen serves the Brampton Filipino community with straightforward, honest cooking. The pancit bihon is made fresh daily with plenty of vegetables and your choice of chicken or pork. The lumpia shanghai is crispy and served with a sweet-chili dip. Good for a quick and satisfying meal.
restaurants.whyFavorite: The lumpia here is some of the crispiest in the area. It's a reliable spot for an affordable Filipino meal when you're in Brampton.

restaurants.name: Dampa Seafood Grill
restaurants.blurb: Seafood-forward Filipino dining in Mississauga — the sinigang sa hipon (shrimp sinigang) is outstanding.
restaurants.description: Dampa takes its name from the open-air seafood shacks of Manila Bay. The Mississauga version brings that spirit with a menu heavy on grilled fish, shrimp sinigang, and kare-kare with oxtail. The crispy pata is fall-off-the-bone and the sauces on the side are house-made.
restaurants.whyFavorite: The sinigang na hipon here uses a tamarind broth so good you'll want to drink it like a soup. One of the better seafood-focused Filipino spots in the GTA.

restaurants.name: Kusina ni Nay
restaurants.blurb: A cosy Mississauga favourite run by a mother-daughter team — the halo-halo here is the stuff of legend.
restaurants.address: 1077 North Service Rd E, Mississauga, ON
restaurants.description: Kusina ni Nay ('Nay's Kitchen') is a warm family restaurant where the matriarch's recipes are front and centre. The halo-halo is generously layered with ube halaya, leche flan, sweetened banana, and two scoops of ube ice cream. Don't skip the dinuguan — it's polarising but perfectly spiced here.
restaurants.whyFavorite: The halo-halo is worth the drive from anywhere in the GTA. Come during summer when the line-up is proof of how good it is.

restaurants.name: Gising Gising
restaurants.blurb: One of Vancouver's most exciting Filipino spots — bold, spicy flavours and a hip neighbourhood vibe.
restaurants.description: Named after the spicy green bean dish, Gising Gising is the kind of restaurant that makes Filipino food feel fresh and relevant. The Bicol Express (coconut milk and chili pork stew) is fiery and complex. The kare-kare is elevated with house-made bagoong. A great natural wine list pairs surprisingly well with Filipino spices.
restaurants.whyFavorite: The most exciting Filipino restaurant in Vancouver right now. The Bicol Express alone is worth the trip, and the seasonal specials always impress.

restaurants.name: Pig Out Manila
restaurants.blurb: Burnaby's premier destination for crispy pork — the lechon kawali here is the real deal.
restaurants.description: If you love anything pork and crispy, Pig Out Manila is your destination. The lechon kawali (twice-cooked crispy pork belly) is golden, crackling, and served with a cane vinegar dip that cuts through the richness. The full lechon on weekends sells out before noon. Come early.
restaurants.whyFavorite: The lechon kawali is the best in Metro Vancouver, full stop. Weekend lechon reservations are essential — don't show up without a heads-up call.

restaurants.name: HapagKainan
restaurants.blurb: Hapag means dining table — and this Richmond spot creates that warm, communal family feel in every dish.
restaurants.description: HapagKainan is a family-run restaurant that keeps things traditional and honest. The sinigang rotates between pork, bangus (milkfish), and shrimp — all equally good. The kare-kare comes with homemade bagoong and a side of rice perfect for soaking up the peanut sauce. Service is always warm and personal.
restaurants.whyFavorite: The most consistent Filipino cooking in Richmond. The sinigang broth is pure comfort and the lumpia shanghai is some of the crispiest you'll find.

restaurants.name: Kumare Filipino Kitchen
restaurants.blurb: Elevated modern Filipino dining in Mount Pleasant — where traditional recipes meet Vancouver's ingredient-driven approach.
restaurants.description: Kumare is Vancouver's answer to high-end Filipino dining. Chef-owners bring a farm-to-table ethos to Filipino classics — the chicken inasal uses free-range birds marinated in calamansi and annatto, and the kare-kare features local oxtail and house-ground peanuts. The halo-halo dessert is a work of art.
restaurants.whyFavorite: For a special occasion Filipino dinner, Kumare is unmatched in Vancouver. The ingredients are impeccable and the tasting menu shows how sophisticated Filipino food can be.

restaurants.name: Wazzup Pinoy Grill
restaurants.blurb: Lively Surrey grill spot with great value — the chicken inasal combo is a steal.
restaurants.description: Wazzup Pinoy Grill is a casual, cheerful spot in Surrey with great bang for your buck. The chicken inasal — marinated in calamansi, annatto, and spices then charcoal-grilled — comes with unlimited rice, which is both generous and dangerous. The pancit canton is loaded with vegetables and hits the spot.
restaurants.whyFavorite: The chicken inasal with unlimited rice combo is one of the best deals in Surrey. Perfect for a quick, satisfying Filipino meal on a budget.

restaurants.name: Bahay Kubo
restaurants.blurb: A neighbourhood turo-turo in Coquitlam with genuine home-style cooking and daily rotating specials.
restaurants.description: Bahay Kubo (named after the iconic Filipino nipa hut) is a point-and-eat style spot that feels like someone's home kitchen opened its doors. Daily specials rotate based on what's fresh — you might find dinuguan one day and binagoongang baboy the next. The sinigang and adobo are always on and always good.
restaurants.whyFavorite: The most authentic turo-turo experience in the Tri-Cities area. Affordable, rotating menu, and genuine home-style cooking that's hard to find.

restaurants.name: Casa Manila
restaurants.blurb: Montreal's most established Filipino restaurant — a warm gathering place for the local community for over 15 years.
restaurants.description: Casa Manila is the landmark Filipino restaurant in Montreal, serving the community since the early 2000s. The menu covers all the classics — adobo, kare-kare, sinigang, and a generous pancit platter. The lechon on weekends (order ahead) is a proper celebration centrepiece. Warm, welcoming atmosphere with Filipino décor.
restaurants.whyFavorite: Casa Manila is where the Montreal Filipino community comes together. The adobo is reliably excellent and the weekend lechon is legendary locally.

restaurants.name: Kusina Filipino Bistro
restaurants.blurb: A modern Filipino bistro in the Plateau that's winning over foodies with bold flavours and a creative menu.
restaurants.description: Kusina Filipino Bistro brings a contemporary approach to the Plateau's restaurant scene. The sisig is their calling card — served in a mini cast-iron pan with a raw egg cracked on top. The kare-kare is made with slow-braised oxtail and comes with excellent homemade bagoong. A good natural wine list adds to the neighbourhood bistro feel.
restaurants.whyFavorite: The Plateau's best-kept Filipino secret. The sisig is exceptional and the halo-halo in summer draws people from across the island.

restaurants.name: Jolly Pinoy
restaurants.blurb: A fun, affordable Filipino spot in Laval serving the North Shore Filipino community.
restaurants.description: Jolly Pinoy is exactly what its name suggests — a cheerful, no-frills spot with good food and great value. The chicken inasal comes with garlic rice and a side of achara (pickled papaya). Lumpia and pancit bihon are always available and reliably fresh. Popular with families on Sunday afternoons.
restaurants.whyFavorite: One of the only Filipino restaurants in Laval, and it delivers. The chicken inasal with garlic rice is comfort food at its best.

restaurants.name: Tindahan Filipino Cafe
restaurants.blurb: Part café, part Filipino grocery — a community hub in NDG with light meals, snacks, and great halo-halo.
restaurants.description: Tindahan (Tagalog for 'store') is part café, part Filipino grocery, and entirely a community hub. You can grab Filipino pantry staples and then sit down for a bowl of pancit, lumpia, or the legendary halo-halo. The ube pandesal (bread rolls) sell out by noon. A great spot for a snack and a Filipino grocery run.
restaurants.whyFavorite: The halo-halo here might be the best in Montreal, and the pandesal fresh from the oven is reason enough to visit in the morning.

restaurants.name: Pinoy BBQ House
restaurants.blurb: Calgary's go-to for charcoal-grilled Filipino BBQ — the chicken inasal and liempo (pork belly) are exceptional.
restaurants.description: Pinoy BBQ House does one thing exceptionally well: charcoal-grilled meats marinated in traditional Filipino spice blends. The chicken inasal is the standout — juicy, smoky, and served with garlic rice. The grilled liempo (pork belly) with sawsawan dipping sauce is equally impressive. NE Calgary's Filipino community counts on this spot for weekend BBQ.
restaurants.whyFavorite: The best charcoal-grilled Filipino meats in Calgary. The chicken inasal comes with unlimited rice and a side of achara that perfectly cuts through the richness.

restaurants.name: Max's Restaurant Calgary
restaurants.blurb: The iconic Philippine chain brings its legendary fried chicken to Calgary — a taste of home for many.
restaurants.description: Max's Restaurant needs no introduction in the Filipino community. The Calgary location serves the same signature crispy fried chicken that made the brand famous across the Philippines. The family platters are great for groups and the halo-halo is a proper version. Weekend brunch draws crowds of Filipino families.
restaurants.whyFavorite: For Filipino-Calgarians, Max's is a pillar of community gathering. The fried chicken combo is consistently excellent and the kare-kare is a reliable crowd-pleaser.

restaurants.name: Filipino Corner Turo-Turo
restaurants.blurb: Steam-table Filipino comfort food in SE Calgary — point and eat, rice and viand, all the greatest hits.
restaurants.description: Filipino Corner is a true turo-turo experience in Calgary's SE. The daily rotating steam table features 8–10 dishes including adobo, sinigang, dinuguan, and seasonal specials. Everything is cooked fresh in the morning and sells out quickly. Bring cash and come before noon to catch the full selection.
restaurants.whyFavorite: The most affordable Filipino meal in Calgary, and the rotating menu keeps it interesting. The dinuguan is properly done and the bulalo on cold days is perfect.

restaurants.name: Halo-Halo Express
restaurants.blurb: Filipino snack spot and dessert bar — the halo-halo is the main event and it does not disappoint.
restaurants.description: Halo-Halo Express started as a dessert kiosk and grew into a proper snack spot. The halo-halo is their flagship — a tall glass of crushed ice, sweet beans, ube halaya, jackfruit, and leche flan topped with ube ice cream. Light meals like pancit and lumpia are available alongside traditional Filipino merienda snacks.
restaurants.whyFavorite: The halo-halo is the best in Calgary and is absolutely essential on a hot summer day. The turon (banana lumpia) dessert is also exceptional.

restaurants.name: Kusina ni Nay Calgary
restaurants.blurb: A welcoming family restaurant serving traditional Filipino cooking with heart in the heart of Calgary.
restaurants.description: Kusina ni Nay brings genuine home-style Filipino cooking to Calgary with a warm, family atmosphere. The kare-kare is slow-cooked and arrives with homemade bagoong. Sinigang comes in pork or seafood variations and the broth is perfectly sour. A great spot for families and groups.
restaurants.whyFavorite: The kare-kare here rivals the best in the country. The bagoong (fermented shrimp paste) is house-made and takes the dish to another level.

restaurants.name: Pinoy Taste
restaurants.blurb: Kelowna's favourite Filipino restaurant — warm service, generous portions, and flavours that feel like home.
restaurants.description: Pinoy Taste is the anchor of Filipino cuisine in the Okanagan. The adobo is made the traditional Tagalog way — braised in soy sauce, vinegar, and garlic until the meat falls apart. The pancit bihon uses fresh rice noodles and is loaded with vegetables. A popular spot for the Filipino community around Kelowna.
restaurants.whyFavorite: For Filipino-Canadians in the Okanagan, Pinoy Taste is a lifeline. The adobo is consistently excellent and the staff make you feel like family.

restaurants.name: Okanagan Filipino Kitchen
restaurants.blurb: A cosy turo-turo style kitchen bringing daily Filipino specials to the Okanagan.
restaurants.description: Okanagan Filipino Kitchen is a modest but beloved spot run by a local Filipino family. The daily steam table features rotating dishes like bulalo, kare-kare, and sinigang. The portions are generous and prices are friendly. A warm, informal space where you're likely to run into the Filipino community.
restaurants.whyFavorite: The bulalo on cold Okanagan nights is perfect comfort food. This family-run spot is the heart of Filipino food culture in Kelowna.

restaurants.name: Kuya's Grill
restaurants.blurb: A charming West Kelowna grill house with excellent inasal and Filipino BBQ skewers.
restaurants.description: Kuya's (kuya means 'big brother' in Filipino) is a casual grill spot in West Kelowna with a focus on BBQ and grilled meats. The chicken inasal is charcoal-grilled and comes with garlic fried rice and achara. The sisig is served sizzling on a cast-iron plate. Popular with both Filipinos and non-Filipinos in the area.
restaurants.whyFavorite: A hidden gem in West Kelowna. The chicken inasal is phenomenal and the owners greet every customer like family.

restaurants.name: Manila Bay Cuisine
restaurants.blurb: Winnipeg's premier Filipino restaurant — the lechon here is roasted in-house and draws the whole community.
restaurants.description: Manila Bay Cuisine is a well-established Filipino restaurant in Winnipeg's North End, a neighbourhood with one of Canada's densest Filipino populations. The lechon is roasted in-house on weekends and often sells out. The sinigang na baboy is perfectly balanced, and the kare-kare with oxtail is outstanding. Large groups are welcome.
restaurants.whyFavorite: The weekend lechon is a community event. Reserve your portion by Friday or you'll miss out. The kare-kare rivals anything in Toronto or Vancouver.

restaurants.name: Pinoy Kitchen Winnipeg
restaurants.blurb: Affordable Filipino comfort food in Winnipeg — the daily lunch specials are the best deal in the city.
restaurants.description: Pinoy Kitchen Winnipeg is a family-run spot that serves the daily Filipino comfort food the community craves. The lunch specials — a viand with rice and soup — are generous and cheap. The pancit canton and lumpia are consistent crowd-pleasers. A friendly, no-frills atmosphere.
restaurants.whyFavorite: The best value Filipino lunch in Winnipeg. The adobo is properly done with lots of garlic, and the afritada (chicken tomato stew) is a hidden gem.

restaurants.name: Kusina ni Lola
restaurants.blurb: Named for grandmother's kitchen — and the food lives up to the name with deeply comforting Filipino home cooking.
restaurants.description: Lola means grandmother in Filipino and this spot honours that name completely. The bulalo (beef bone marrow soup) is slow-cooked for hours and arrives with tender shank and marrow you can spread on bread. The kare-kare is made from an old family recipe. Everything here is slow and deliberate, and you taste the difference.
restaurants.whyFavorite: The bulalo here is arguably the best in Manitoba — deeply rich, slow-cooked, and absolutely comforting. Come hungry and bring the whole family.

restaurants.name: Inihaw Express
restaurants.blurb: A fast, casual Filipino grill spot in Winnipeg with excellent charcoal-grilled meats and friendly service.
restaurants.description: Inihaw (meaning 'grilled') Express is a casual Filipino grill with a streamlined menu focused on what it does best: charcoal-grilled meats. The chicken inasal combo with garlic rice and sawsawan is the bestseller. The lechon kawali (crispy braised pork belly) is golden and spectacular. Fast service and great value.
restaurants.whyFavorite: The lechon kawali and chicken inasal combo is one of the most satisfying Filipino meals in Winnipeg. The sawsawan dipping sauce is house-made and delicious.

restaurants.name: Pinoy House Edmonton
restaurants.blurb: A beloved Edmonton institution serving classic Filipino cooking to the community for over a decade.
restaurants.description: Pinoy House Edmonton is a well-established restaurant that has been feeding the Filipino-Edmontonian community for years. The adobo is classic and reliable, the sinigang comes in three variants, and the lechon (on weekends) is the star of the show. The warm, family-style dining room makes every visit feel like a celebration.
restaurants.whyFavorite: One of Edmonton's best Filipino restaurants. The weekend lechon draws the community together and the adobo is a gold standard rendition.

restaurants.name: Lutong Pinoy NW
restaurants.blurb: NW Edmonton's turo-turo gem — honest, affordable Filipino home cooking with daily rotating specials.
restaurants.description: Lutong Pinoy NW is a community-focused turo-turo that serves the large Filipino population in northwest Edmonton. Daily specials rotate based on what's fresh, but the pancit bihon and lumpia are always on the menu. Affordable lunch combos and a warm, casual atmosphere make this a regular haunt for local Filipino families.
restaurants.whyFavorite: The best deal for Filipino food in NW Edmonton. The pancit bihon is fresh daily and the rotating specials are always worth trying.

restaurants.name: Barrio Edmonton
restaurants.blurb: Modern Filipino dining on Whyte Ave — bold flavours, great cocktails, and a lively atmosphere.
restaurants.description: Barrio Edmonton is the city's most exciting Filipino dining spot, bringing a contemporary approach to classics. The sisig is served tableside on a sizzling skillet with house-made atchara. The kare-kare uses premium oxtail and is paired with a housemade shrimp paste. The cocktail menu draws on Filipino flavours like calamansi and tamarind.
restaurants.whyFavorite: Edmonton's best modern Filipino restaurant. The sisig is exceptional and the atmosphere on Whyte Ave is perfect for a fun night out.

restaurants.name: Filipino Food House
restaurants.blurb: A Mill Woods staple with generous portions of Filipino comfort food at prices the whole family can afford.
restaurants.description: Filipino Food House serves the large Filipino community in Mill Woods with generous portions of home-style cooking. The bulalo is a community favourite, and the crispy pata (deep-fried pork knuckle) is served with vinegar and soy dipping sauce. The sinigang broth is tangy and warming, perfect for Edmonton winters.
restaurants.whyFavorite: The crispy pata here is worth the trip. Generously portioned and affordable, it's a go-to for Filipino comfort food in Mill Woods.

restaurants.name: Manila Gardens
restaurants.blurb: A spacious West Edmonton restaurant perfect for large family gatherings with a full classic Filipino menu.
restaurants.description: Manila Gardens is a spacious restaurant well-suited for big family gatherings and celebrations. The full menu covers all Filipino classics — kare-kare, lechon kawali, pancit, and more. The lechon kawali is particularly good — crispy, golden, and served with a banana ketchup dip. A reliable and comfortable spot.
restaurants.whyFavorite: The best restaurant in West Edmonton for large group Filipino dinners. The lechon kawali is consistently excellent and the kare-kare is a standout.

restaurants.name: Manila Garden Ottawa
restaurants.blurb: Ottawa's most-loved Filipino restaurant — the community gathering spot for celebrations, bayanihan, and great food.
restaurants.description: Manila Garden Ottawa is the anchor of Filipino cuisine in the nation's capital. The menu is comprehensive and reliably excellent — from kare-kare and sinigang to the classic pork adobo. The restaurant frequently hosts community events and celebrations, and the warm staff make everyone feel welcome.
restaurants.whyFavorite: The Filipino community's home base in Ottawa. The kare-kare is superb and the adobo is as good as it gets outside a Filipino home kitchen.

restaurants.name: Kuya's Kitchen
restaurants.blurb: A tiny but mighty home-style kitchen in Gloucester — Kuya's is where Ottawa Filipinos go when they miss home.
restaurants.description: Kuya's Kitchen (kuya = elder brother) is a small, unpretentious spot run by a Filipino family. The rotating turo-turo style menu features daily specials based on what's fresh and in season. The bulalo is their signature — slow-cooked overnight for a deeply rich broth. Cash only, limited seating, totally worth it.
restaurants.whyFavorite: The bulalo here is among the best in Ontario, with a broth so rich it coats the spoon. A true community hidden gem in Gloucester.

restaurants.name: Pinoy Express Ottawa
restaurants.blurb: A casual Filipino grill in Orleans with fast service and great grilled meats — the inasal is the one to order.
restaurants.description: Pinoy Express serves the growing Filipino community in Orleans with a focused menu of grilled meats and Filipino comfort food. The chicken inasal combo with garlic rice is the bestseller. The lechon kawali is crispy and comes with a sawsawan of soy, vinegar, and chili. Quick service and a bright, cheerful atmosphere.
restaurants.whyFavorite: The most convenient and consistent Filipino spot in Orleans. The chicken inasal is properly marinated and the crispy lumpia is always fresh.
